## Biography of Chaim Malinowitz

**Rabbi Chaim Zev Malinowitz** (Hebrew: ×××× ×××× ××××¥; 1952 â November 21, 2019) was a prominent Haredi rabbi, dayan (rabbinical court judge), and Talmudic scholar, best known as the general editor of the landmark 73-volume **Schottenstein Edition of the Babylonian Talmud** published by ArtScroll.

### Early Life and Education
Malinowitz was born in 1952 on the Lower East Side of New York City. His father, **Rabbi Avrohom Aharon Malinowitz**, had been a student of the renowned Rabbi Aharon Kotler in Kletsk, Poland. Chaim was a gifted student at the Rabbi Jacob Joseph School. After his bar mitzvah, he was accepted to the **Talmudical Yeshiva of Philadelphia**, where he skipped two grades and entered the beth midrash program under Rabbi Mendel Kaplan. His devotion to study was such that he began waking at 4 a.m. daily for vasikin (pre-dawn prayer), a practice he maintained his entire life.

He went on to study at **Yeshivas Iyun HaTalmud** in Monsey, New York, under Rabbi Abba Berman, a principal disciple of Rabbi Yerucham Levovitz. He remained there for six years until his marriage in 1976, after which he entered the kollel. In 1980, Rabbi Berman appointed him rosh kollel when Berman himself made aliyah to Israel. He received his rabbinic ordination (semikhah) from **Rabbi Moshe Feinstein**, one of the foremost halakhic authorities of the 20th century.

### Rabbinic Career
After ordination, Malinowitz served as a **dayan** (rabbinical court judge) for the rabbinical court of Kollel HaRabbanim in Monsey. In this role, he became recognized as a **world authority on gittin** (Jewish divorce law) and dealt with complex cases of agunos (chained women whose husbands refused to grant a religious divorce). He notably **opposed the 1992 New York Get Law**, arguing that its coercive penalties could halakhically invalidate all divorces in New York. Rabbi Shlomo Zalman Auerbach ultimately agreed with his position, which eroded support for the law.

In 1992, Malinowitz was appointed â alongside Rabbi Yisrael Simcha Schorr â as **general editor of the Schottenstein Edition of the Babylonian Talmud**. This monumental English-language translation and commentary spanned 73 volumes and took 15 years to complete. Malinowitz was responsible for approving **every single line and footnote** of both the English and Hebrew editions. According to senior editor Rabbi Eliezer Herzka, Malinowitz's role was to "learn through the sugya with the ArtScroll commentary to probe it to find its weak points." His encyclopedic knowledge of the Talmud ensured that the commentary adequately reflected the views of classic Talmudic commentators across the entire corpus. He also contributed to ArtScroll's elucidated Mishnah project.

In 1997, Malinowitz made aliyah (immigrated to Israel) and began teaching at **Yeshivat Aish HaTorah** in Jerusalem. In 2002, he became the **Rav (rabbi) of Beis Tefillah Yonah Avraham** in Ramat Beit Shemesh, an English-speaking congregation serving Anglophone immigrants, a position he held for 17 years until his passing. He introduced a rich program of shiurim (classes) at multiple levels for men, women, and children, and studied one-on-one with many congregants.

Notably, Malinowitz **broke with his Haredi colleagues** by opposing the ban on Natan Slifkin's books about Torah and science, demonstrating his intellectual independence and commitment to truth as he saw it.

### Personal Qualities
Malinowitz was described as fluent in all areas of the Talmud, halakha (Jewish law), and hashkafa (Orthodox Jewish worldview). He combined this extraordinary scholarship with "fearlessness" in acting on his convictions, while also being known as a warm, friendly, and "down to earth" personality who genuinely cared for others.

### Personal Life
Malinowitz married **Simi Maza**, daughter of Rabbi Dovber Maza, in 1976. They had **ten children** â six sons and four daughters. After making aliyah, the family resided in Jerusalem.

He died in Jerusalem on **November 21, 2019**, at the age of 67, and was buried in the Rehovot cemetery near his parents.

### Selected Writings
- "The New York State Get Bill and its Halachic Ramifications" (Jewish Law Articles)
- "Pitfalls to Avoid in Obtaining a Get" (Jewish Action, Winter 1999)

[Source: Wikipedia â "Chaim Malinowitz"; references include Mishpacha magazine, The Jewish Press, Hamodia, and others]
